concepts.concept_name: Definition and Parts of a Circle
concepts.lesson: A circle is the set of all points in a plane that are at a fixed distance from a fixed point called the center. Key parts include radius (distance from center to any point on circle), diameter (twice the radius), chord (line segment joining two points on the circle), and circumference (perimeter of circle). Understanding these basic elements is essential for all geometric properties and calculations.
concepts.real_example: A circle with radius 7 cm has diameter = 14 cm.
key_points: Radius = center to any point on circle
Chord = line segment joining two points

concepts.lesson: A tangent to a circle is a line that touches the circle at exactly one point. The radius drawn to the point of contact is perpendicular to the tangent. Tangents are used in constructing geometric figures, solving angle problems, and proving properties related to circles.
concepts.real_example: For a circle with center O, tangent at point P is perpendicular to OP.
key_points: Touches circle at only one point
Radius to point of contact ⊥ tangent

concepts.lesson: A secant is a line that intersects the circle at two points. A chord is a segment connecting two points on the circle. Equal chords of a circle are equidistant from the center, and perpendicular from center bisects the chord. These properties help in constructing circles and solving geometry problems.
concepts.real_example: Chord AB = CD implies distance from center to AB = distance from center to CD.
key_points: Chord = line segment joining two points

concepts.concept_name: Angle Subtended by Chord at Center
concepts.lesson: The angle subtended by a chord at the center is twice the angle subtended at any point on the remaining part of the circle. This property is used in solving problems related to cyclic quadrilaterals, arcs, and circle theorems.
concepts.real_example: Chord AB subtends 60° at center O and 30° at point C on the circle.
key_points: Angle at center = 2 × angle at circumference

concepts.lesson: A quadrilateral is cyclic if all its vertices lie on a circle. Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°. This property is used in solving problems involving tangents, chords, and inscribed angles.
concepts.real_example: Quadrilateral ABCD inscribed in a circle has ∠A + ∠C = 180°.

concepts.lesson: If a tangent and a secant (or two tangents) are drawn from an external point, certain lengths satisfy specific relations. For a tangent PT and secant PAB from point P, PT² = PA × PB. This theorem is useful in advanced geometry problems and competitions.
concepts.real_example: From point P outside circle, tangent PT = √(PA × PB) where PA and PB are lengths of secant.
key_points: Applies to tangents and secants from same point

concepts.lesson: The angle formed between a tangent and a chord through the point of contact is equal to the angle subtended by the chord at the opposite side of the circle. This is a key property for solving angle problems in circle geometry.
concepts.real_example: If tangent at A and chord AB, ∠TAB = ∠ACB.
key_points: Angle between tangent and chord = angle subtended by chord opposite side
Helps in solving inscribed angle problems

concepts.lesson: Key theorems include: angles in the same segment are equal, opposite angles of cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°, tangents from external point are equal, and perpendicular from center bisects chord. These theorems form the basis for proofs and problem-solving involving circles.
concepts.real_example: Two tangents from external point P to circle with center O are equal in length.
key_points: Opposite angles of cyclic quadrilateral sum 180°

concepts.lesson: An arc is a part of the circle, and a sector is the region bounded by two radii and the arc. Length of arc = (θ/360) × 2πr, area of sector = (θ/360) × πr², where θ is central angle. These formulas are essential for solving practical problems involving circle measurements.
concepts.real_example: Circle radius 7 cm, sector with angle 60°, area = (60/360) × π × 7² = 25.4 cm².

concepts.lesson: A segment is the region bounded by a chord and corresponding arc. Area of segment = area of sector − area of triangle formed by chord and radii. This concept is used in advanced geometry and engineering applications.
concepts.real_example: Circle radius 10 cm, chord 12 cm, area of segment = area of sector − area of triangle.
key_points: Segment = area between chord and arc
